questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
questions.question: What is the name of the line segment from the center of a regular polygon perpendicular to one of its sides?
questions.hint: This segment is used to calculate the area and is perpendicular to a side.
questions.explanation: The apothem is the perpendicular distance from the center to any side of a regular polygon. Option B (radius) goes to a vertex, not perpendicular to a side.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
source_trace.content_anchor: line segment from the center perpendicular to the sides of a regular polygon is called an apothem

explanations_tiered.definition: An apothem is a perpendicular segment from the center of a regular polygon to the midpoint of any side.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The apothem is the radius of the inscribed circle (incircle) and is used in area calculations: \(A = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{apothem} \times \text{perimeter}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The apothem is like a line from the center straight to the middle of a side, at a right angle to that side.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In a regular polygon, the apothem is the distance from the center to any side, measured perpendicularly. It is also the radius of the incircle.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For an \(n\)-gon inscribed in a circle of radius \(r\), the apothem is \(a = r \cos(180^\circ/n)\). It bisects the central angle and side.
reasoning_steps: Identify the center of the regular polygon.
Draw a perpendicular from the center to any side.
The length of this perpendicular is the apothem.
misconceptions: Some confuse apothem with radius (distance to vertex).
Others think apothem is the side length; it is perpendicular to the side, not equal to it.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Apothem: perpendicular from center to a side. Used in area: \(A = \frac{1}{2} a P\).

glossary_entry.definition: The perpendicular distance from the center of a regular polygon to any of its sides.

flashcard.front: What is the apothem of a regular polygon?
flashcard.back: The perpendicular distance from the center to any side. It is the radius of the incircle.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does apothem mean in a regular polygon?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The perpendicular distance from the center to a side; also the incircle radius.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The perpendicular distance from the center of a regular polygon to any of its sides.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw a regular hexagon and show both radius (to vertex) and apothem (to side midpoint).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: solve problems on area and perimeter of regular polygons
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The apothem is the perpendicular from center to a side; radius goes to a vertex.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
questions.question: What is the measure of each central angle in a regular octagon?
questions.hint: Central angles sum to \(360^\circ\) and are all equal in a regular polygon.
questions.explanation: Each central angle = \(360^\circ / n = 360^\circ / 8 = 45^\circ\). Option B is for a decagon; option D for a hexagon.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
source_trace.content_anchor: measure of each central angle of n-sided regular polygon is 360°/n

explanations_tiered.definition: A central angle in a regular polygon is the angle formed by two radii drawn to consecutive vertices.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: All central angles are equal and sum to \(360^\circ\). For an octagon (\(n=8\)), each is \(360^\circ/8 = 45^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine cutting a regular octagon like a pizza into 8 equal slices. The tip angle of each slice is the central angle: \(45^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Central angle = \(360^\circ / n\). For \(n=8\), \(360/8 = 45^\circ\). This is independent of side length.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The central angle subtends one side of the polygon. In a regular \(n\)-gon, the \(n\) congruent isosceles triangles formed have vertex angle \(360^\circ/n\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Recall formula for central angle: \(360^\circ / n\).
reasoning_steps: Remember that central angles sum to a full circle \(360^\circ\).
Divide \(360^\circ\) by the number of sides \(n\).
Compute \(360 \div 8 = 45\).

formula_analysis.name: Central angle of regular polygon
formula_analysis.derivation: Equal angles around the center sum to 360°.

misconceptions: Using \(180^\circ/n\) instead of \(360^\circ/n\).
Confusing central angle with interior angle: interior is much larger.

glossary_entry.definition: The angle at the center of a regular polygon formed by two radii to consecutive vertices.

flashcard.front: What is the central angle of a regular octagon?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find the central angle of a regular 15-gon.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Angle at the center formed by radii to two adjacent vertices.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to interior angle: interior + central ≠ 180 generally.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: find the measure of each interior or exterior angle of a regular polygon
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Central angle = \(360^\circ/8 = 45^\circ\). Option B is for decagon (36°), D for hexagon (60°).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.1 Sum of Interior Angles of a Convex Polygons
questions.structural_anchor: Deriving sum of the interior angles of a polygon
questions.question: A convex polygon has 9 sides. What is the sum of its interior angles?
questions.explanation: Sum = \((9-2) \times 180^\circ = 7 \times 180^\circ = 1260^\circ\). Option B is for octagon; option D would require \(n=11\).

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.1 Sum of Interior Angles of a Convex Polygons
source_trace.structural_element: Deriving sum of the interior angles of a polygon
source_trace.content_anchor: sum of measure of interior angles of n sided polygon is equal to (n-2)×180°

explanations_tiered.definition: The sum of interior angles of an \(n\)-gon is \((n-2) \times 180^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a nonagon (\(n=9\)), substitute into the formula: \((9-2) \times 180^\circ = 1260^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: A 9-sided shape can be split into 7 triangles (9 minus 2). Multiply 7 by 180 to get \(1260^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using triangulation: \(n-2 = 7\) triangles, each \(180^\circ\). Total interior sum = \(1260^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Inductive proof: adding a triangle to a polygon increases side count by 1 and angle sum by \(180^\circ\). Starting from triangle (3 sides, \(180^\circ\)), 9 sides gives \(180^\circ + 6 \times 180^\circ = 1260^\circ\).
reasoning_steps: Identify number of sides \(n=9\).
Compute \(n-2 = 7\).
Multiply \(7 \times 180^\circ\).
misconceptions: Using \(n \times 180^\circ\) gives \(1620^\circ\) (too high).
Using \((n-1) \times 180^\circ\) gives \(1440^\circ\) (one extra triangle).

glossary_entry.definition: A polygon with nine sides and nine vertices.

flashcard.front: What is the sum of interior angles of a nonagon (9 sides)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find interior angle sum for a 9-sided polygon.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Have students draw diagonals from one vertex to verify 7 triangles.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: solve problems on area and perimeter of regular polygons
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. \((9-2) \times 180 = 1260^\circ\). Option B is octagon (8 sides), C would require 10 sides.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.2 Sum of Exterior Angles of a Convex Polygons
questions.structural_anchor: Sum of the exterior angles of a polygon
questions.question: How does the sum of exterior angles of a convex polygon change as the number of sides increases?
questions.hint: Think about the total turning when walking around any polygon.
questions.explanation: The sum of exterior angles (one per vertex) is always \(360^\circ\) for any convex polygon, regardless of \(n\). Options A and B incorrectly imply dependence on \(n\).

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.2 Sum of Exterior Angles of a Convex Polygons
source_trace.structural_element: Sum of the exterior angles of a polygon
source_trace.content_anchor: For any n-sided polygon, the sum of the measure of exterior angles of a polygon is 360°

explanations_tiered.definition: The sum of exterior angles of any convex polygon, taking one per vertex, is always \(360^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This constant sum holds for triangles, quadrilaterals, and all convex polygons regardless of side count.
explanations_tiered.beginner: No matter how many sides a shape has, the total turn you make walking around it is always one full circle: \(360^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Increasing \(n\) decreases each exterior angle (\(360/n\)) but the sum remains \(n \times (360/n) = 360^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Algebraic cancellation: sum(ext) = \(n \times 180^\circ - (n-2) \times 180^\circ = 360^\circ\). The \(n\) terms cancel.
reasoning_steps: Recall that each exterior angle + interior angle = \(180^\circ\).
Sum of all linear pairs = \(n \times 180^\circ\).
Subtract interior sum \((n-2) \times 180^\circ\) to get \(360^\circ\) constant.
misconceptions: Believing that more sides means larger total exterior sum.
Thinking that exterior sum = \(n \times 180^\circ\) (that's the sum of linear pairs).

glossary_entry.definition: The total measure of one exterior angle at each vertex of any convex polygon, always \(360^\circ\).

flashcard.front: What happens to the sum of exterior angles if you double the number of sides?
flashcard.back: It stays \(360^\circ\). The sum is constant for all convex polygons.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: True or false: The sum of exterior angles increases with more sides.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: False. It is always \(360^\circ\) for any convex polygon.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The constant \(360^\circ\) total of exterior angles, one per vertex, for any convex polygon.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Ask students to calculate for triangle, square, pentagon — all yield 360°.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: find the measure of each interior or exterior angle of a regular polygon
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. The sum is constant at \(360^\circ\), independent of the number of sides.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
questions.subsection: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle
questions.structural_anchor: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle
questions.question: For a regular \(n\)-gon inscribed in a circle of radius \(r\), what is the formula for the side length \(s\)?
questions.hint: Consider the right triangle formed by the apothem, radius, and half the side.
questions.explanation: Half of the side is \(r \sin(180^\circ/n)\), so \(s = 2r \sin(180^\circ/n)\). Option A gives the apothem; option C is half the side.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle
source_trace.structural_element: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle

explanations_tiered.definition: The side length of a regular \(n\)-gon inscribed in a circle of radius \(r\) is \(s = 2r \sin(180^\circ/n)\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: From the isosceles triangle with sides \(r, r, s\), the central angle is \(360^\circ/n\). Half of that gives a right triangle where half-side = \(r \sin(180^\circ/n)\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine cutting the central angle in half; you get a right triangle. The opposite side (half the polygon side) is \(r \times \sin(\text{half-angle})\). Double it for whole side.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In right triangle with hypotenuse \(r\) and angle \(180^\circ/n\), opposite side = \(r \sin(180^\circ/n)\). That is half the polygon side, so \(s = 2r \sin(180^\circ/n)\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derivation: chord length formula for angle \(\theta = 360^\circ/n\): chord = \(2r \sin(\theta/2) = 2r \sin(180^\circ/n)\).
reasoning_steps: Draw radius to two adjacent vertices, central angle = \(360^\circ/n\).
Bisect that angle: right triangle with angle \(180^\circ/n\).
Opposite side = \(r \sin(180^\circ/n)\) = half side length.
Multiply by 2 for full side.

formula_analysis.name: Side length of inscribed regular polygon
formula_analysis.derivation: Chord length from central angle θ = 360°/n

misconceptions: Using cosine instead of sine gives apothem, not side.
Forgetting factor of 2: half-side is \(r \sin(\theta/2)\), side is \(2r \sin(\theta/2)\).

glossary_entry.definition: A line segment whose endpoints lie on a circle.

flashcard.front: What is the side length formula for a regular \(n\)-gon inscribed in a circle radius \(r\)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Formula for side length \(s\) of a regular \(n\)-gon in a circle radius \(r\)?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Line segment connecting two points on a circle; for regular polygons, side = chord of central angle.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Derive geometrically: drop perpendicular from center to side to form right triangle.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: solve problems on area and perimeter of regular polygons
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Half-side = \(r \sin(180^\circ/n)\), so full side = \(2r \sin(180^\circ/n)\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
questions.subsection: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle
questions.structural_anchor: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle
questions.question: A regular hexagon is inscribed in a circle of radius 10 cm. What is its perimeter?
questions.hint: For a regular hexagon, side length equals radius.
questions.explanation: For a regular hexagon, side length \(s = r = 10\text{ cm}\). Perimeter = \(6 \times 10 = 60\text{ cm}\). Option C (50) would be 5 cm per side, incorrect.

calculation_steps: For hexagon: side length = radius = 10 cm

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle
source_trace.structural_element: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle
source_trace.content_anchor: regular hexagon inscribed: s = r, P = 6r

explanations_tiered.definition: A regular hexagon inscribed in a circle has side length equal to the radius.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Central angle is \(60^\circ\), forming equilateral triangles; thus \(s = r\). Perimeter = \(6r = 60\text{ cm}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: A hexagon inside a circle makes 6 equilateral triangles. Each triangle side equals the circle's radius, so hexagon side = 10 cm. Perimeter = 6 × 10 = 60 cm.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Central angle of hexagon = \(360^\circ/6 = 60^\circ\). The triangle formed by two radii and one side is equilateral, so \(s = r = 10\text{ cm}\). Perimeter = \(6 \times 10 = 60\text{ cm}\).
worked_solution: Step 1: For a regular hexagon, \(s = r\) (equilateral triangles).
reasoning_steps: Identify \(n=6\) for hexagon.
Recall property: inscribed regular hexagon side equals radius.
Multiply side length by 6.
misconceptions: Using \(s = 2r \sin(30^\circ) = r\) is correct but some forget hexagon special case and use wrong \(n\).
Confusing perimeter with area: area would involve apothem.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Inscribed regular hexagon: side = radius, perimeter = 6r.

glossary_entry.definition: A six-sided regular polygon; when inscribed in a circle, side length equals the radius.

flashcard.front: A regular hexagon is inscribed in a circle of radius 5 cm. What is its perimeter?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Inscribed regular hexagon, radius 10 cm: perimeter?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Six-sided regular polygon; inscribed side equals circumradius.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show that central angle 60° creates equilateral triangles.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: solve problems on area and perimeter of regular polygons
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. For a regular hexagon, side = radius = 10 cm, so perimeter = 60 cm. Option C (50) might come from using 5 cm side incorrectly.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
questions.subsection: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle
questions.structural_anchor: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle
questions.question: A regular octagon is inscribed in a circle of radius 8 cm. Which expression gives its perimeter?
questions.explanation: \(P = n \times 2r \sin(180^\circ/n) = 8 \times 2 \times 8 \times \sin(22.5^\circ) = 16 \times 8 \times \sin(22.5^\circ)\). Option B is just one side.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle
source_trace.structural_element: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle

explanations_tiered.definition: Perimeter of regular \(n\)-gon inscribed in radius \(r\) is \(P = 2nr \sin(180^\circ/n)\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The perimeter formula multiplies number of sides by side length: \(8 \times [2 \times 8 \times \sin(22.5^\circ)] = 16 \times 8 \times \sin(22.5^\circ)\).
reasoning_steps: Recall perimeter formula for regular inscribed polygon.
Substitute \(n=8\) and \(r=8\).
Compute \(180^\circ/n = 22.5^\circ\).
Simplify coefficient: \(2 \times 8 \times 8 = 128 = 16 \times 8\).
misconceptions: Using \(2r \sin(180^\circ/n)\) as perimeter (that's side length, not perimeter).
Using \(\sin(45^\circ)\) instead of \(22.5^\circ\) because \(360/8 = 45\), but formula uses half that.

flashcard.front: Perimeter formula for regular octagon inscribed in circle radius r?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Write the perimeter formula for a regular octagon inscribed in radius r.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Eight-sided regular polygon; perimeter depends on circumradius.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: solve problems on area and perimeter of regular polygons

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
questions.question: A regular polygon has a circumcircle of radius \(R\) and an incircle of radius \(a\). What is the relationship between \(R\) and \(a\) for any regular polygon?
questions.hint: The circumradius goes to vertices; the apothem (incircle radius) is perpendicular to sides.
questions.explanation: The circumradius \(R\) (to a vertex) is always longer than the apothem \(a\) (perpendicular to a side), except for degenerate cases. Option B is correct. For a square, \(R = a\sqrt{2} > a\).

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
source_trace.content_anchor: radius of the incircle is the apothem; circumradius to vertex longer

explanations_tiered.definition: Circumradius \(R\) is the distance from center to a vertex; apothem \(a\) is distance from center to a side.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For any regular polygon with \(n \geq 3\), the apothem is \(a = R \cos(180^\circ/n)\). Since \(\cos(180^\circ/n) < 1\), \(a < R\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The distance to a corner is longer than the distance to the middle of a side, so \(R > a\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In the right triangle formed by radius, apothem, and half-side, the radius is the hypotenuse, so \(R > a\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: From formula \(a = R \cos(180^\circ/n)\), and \(0 < \cos(180^\circ/n) < 1\) for \(n \geq 3\), therefore \(a < R\).
reasoning_steps: Recognize that radius (to vertex) and apothem (to side) are related by cosine.
Note that cosine of any acute angle is less than 1.
Conclude \(a = R \cos(\theta) < R\).
misconceptions: Thinking \(R = a\) for all regular polygons (only true as \(n \to \infty\), circle).
Assuming \(R < a\) because apothem might seem 'inside' more.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Circumradius > apothem for any regular polygon with \(n \geq 3\).

glossary_entry.definition: Radius of the circumscribed circle (distance from center to vertex).

flashcard.front: Which is larger: circumradius \(R\) or apothem \(a\) of a regular polygon?
flashcard.back: \(R > a\). The apothem is the perpendicular distance to a side, shorter than the slant to a vertex.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Compare circumradius and apothem for a regular octagon.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Circumradius > apothem. Apothem = R cos(22.5°), cos(22.5°) ≈ 0.924 < 1.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Distance from center to vertex; always greater than apothem.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw the right triangle: R is hypotenuse, a is adjacent side to half-central angle.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: solve problems on area and perimeter of regular polygons
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Since apothem = R cos(180°/n) and cos(θ) < 1 for 0<θ<90°, we have a < R.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.3 Measures of Each Interior Angle and Exterior Angle of a Regular Polygon
questions.question: A student incorrectly calculates the interior angle of a regular decagon as \(150^\circ\). What error was likely made?
questions.hint: The correct interior angle for a decagon is \(144^\circ\); \(1440^\circ\) is the sum of all interior angles.
questions.explanation: The student computed \((n-2) \times 180^\circ = 1440^\circ\) but forgot to divide by \(n=10\). Option B gives exterior angle; C gives \(180^\circ\); D gives \(120^\circ\).

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.3 Measures of Each Interior Angle and Exterior Angle of a Regular Polygon

explanations_tiered.definition: Each interior angle of a regular \(n\)-gon is \(\frac{(n-2) \times 180^\circ}{n}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a decagon (\(n=10\)), correct angle = \(144^\circ\). \(1440^\circ\) is the total sum, not the per-angle measure.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The student found the total of all angles (1440°) but forgot to split it equally among the 10 corners.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(1440^\circ\) equals \((10-2) \times 180^\circ\), which is the sum of interior angles. Dividing by 10 gives the correct \(144^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Common procedural error: compute total sum but stop there, omitting division by \(n\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Correct formula: each interior = \((n-2) \times 180^\circ / n\).
reasoning_steps: Recall formula for each interior angle.
Compute total sum first if needed, then divide by n.
Identify that 1440° is sum, not per angle.
misconceptions: Stopping at total sum without dividing.
Confusing sum formula with per-angle formula.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Each interior angle = total sum / n. Don't forget division!

flashcard.front: A student says each interior angle of a regular decagon is 1440°. What mistake?
flashcard.back: They computed total sum (1440°) but forgot to divide by 10.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why might a student get 1440° as the interior angle of a decagon?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: They computed total interior sum but forgot to divide by number of sides (10).
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Don't forget: divide total sum by n.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Highlight that total sum vs per-angle is a common trap.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: find the measure of each interior or exterior angle of a regular polygon
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The student used the sum formula but omitted division by n; 1440° is the total sum for a decagon, not each angle.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.1 Sum of Interior Angles of a Convex Polygons
questions.question: Which of the following polygons is always convex?
questions.hint: Consider which polygon cannot have a reflex interior angle.
questions.explanation: Every triangle is convex because the sum of three angles is 180°, so each must be less than 180°. Quadrilaterals and higher can be concave (e.g., dart shape).

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.1 Sum of Interior Angles of a Convex Polygons
source_trace.content_anchor: triangle is the simplest polygon; all triangles are convex

explanations_tiered.definition: A convex polygon has all interior angles less than \(180^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A triangle's angles sum to \(180^\circ\), so each must be less than \(180^\circ\), making every triangle convex. Quadrilaterals and higher can have a reflex angle.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You cannot draw a triangle with a dent (concave) because three sides always make a simple, outward shape.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In a triangle, each interior angle < \(180^\circ\) automatically because sum is 180. For quadrilaterals, one angle can exceed 180 (concave).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Proof: if any angle of a triangle were ≥180°, the sum would exceed 180°, contradiction. Thus all triangles are convex.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of convex: all interior angles < 180°.
Triangle angle sum = 180°, so each angle must be < 180°.
Therefore, any triangle satisfies convex condition.
misconceptions: Some think a quadrilateral can be made with all angles <180 but still concave? No, if all angles <180 it's convex. But quadrilaterals can have one angle >180, making them concave. Triangles cannot.
Confusing 'always convex' with 'sometimes convex'.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: All triangles are convex. Other polygons may be concave.

flashcard.front: Which polygon is always convex: triangle, quadrilateral, pentagon, or hexagon?
flashcard.back: Triangle. A triangle cannot have an interior angle ≥ 180° because sum would exceed 180°.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Triangle angles sum to 180°, so each must be less than 180°, satisfying convex definition.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show a concave quadrilateral (arrowhead) to contrast.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Triangles cannot have an angle ≥180° because sum is 180°, so all triangles are convex. Quadrilaterals and higher can be concave.
